revision.text:
[[ko:낙랑 고분]] {{ill|Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster|ja|楽浪古墳群}} '''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡}}, a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島}} by the {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢|lt=Han dynasty|lt_ja=漢王朝}}’s {{ill|Emperor Wu of Han|ja|武帝 (漢)|lt=Emperor Wu of Han|lt_ja=武帝}} in {{ill|108 BC|ja|紀元前108th year }}.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|{{ill|Encyclopedia of the World|ja|世界大百科事典}}}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡}} corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper={{ill|Dong-A Ilbo|ja|東亜日報}}|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市}} and the {{ill|Lelang area|ja|楽浪区域}}, particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>
== Overview == The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳}} style around the turn from the {{ill|1st century|ja|1世紀}} to the {{ill|2nd century|ja|2世紀}}. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the {{ill|2nd century|ja|2世紀}}, they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|lt=Lelang|lt_ja=楽浪}} and {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡}}.<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after {{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year }}, when {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} came to dominate {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡}} and {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡}}. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in {{ill|South Hwanghae Province|ja|黄海南道}}’s {{ill|Sinchon County|ja|信川郡}} reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in {{ill|404 AD|ja|404th year }} when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher={{ill|National History Compilation Committee|ja|国史編纂委員会}}|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>
Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Wu of Han|ja|武帝 (漢)|lt=Han's Emperor Wu|lt_ja=漢の武帝}}, four commanderies—{{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|lt=Lelang|lt_ja=楽浪}}, {{ill|Xuantu Commandery|ja|玄菟郡|lt=Xuantu|lt_ja=玄菟}}, {{ill|Lintun Commandery|ja|臨屯郡|lt=Lintun|lt_ja=臨屯}}, and {{ill|Zhenfan Commandery|ja|真番郡|lt=Zhenfan|lt_ja=真番}}—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道}} to the {{ill|Yellow Sea|ja|黄海}} and {{ill|Pyongan Province|ja|平安道}}. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, {{ill|Gongsun Kang|ja|公孫康}} established his power in the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東}}, Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡}}. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)|lt=Wei|lt_ja=魏}}, the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Jin dynasty|ja|晋 (王朝)|lt=Jin|lt_ja=晋}}. Meanwhile, with the rise of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|lt=Goryeo|lt_ja=高麗}} in the north and the growing power of {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from {{ill|China|ja|支那}} were stationed, and many {{ill|Han people|ja|漢民族}} settled there, importing Han {{ill|culture|ja|文化}}; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant}} and {{ill|farmers|ja|農民}} migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join {{ill|Murong Chui|ja|慕容廆}} in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興}} era ({{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year }}). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}, which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of {{ill|King Jangsu|ja|長寿王}} after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章}} by {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|lt=Gaozong of Tang|lt_ja=唐の高宗}} of the {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the {{ill|Andong Protectorate|ja|安東都護府}}, difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東}} shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher={{ill|Toyo Bunko|ja|東洋文庫}}|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島}}, namely in the area of the former {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡}}, as well as in the {{ill|Hwangae Area|ja|黄海南道|lt=Hwangae Area|lt_ja=黄海道地域}} where the later-severed {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡}} was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within {{ill|Sunchon City|ja|順川市}}. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from {{ill|North Korea|ja|朝鮮民主主義人民共和国|lt=North Korea|lt_ja=北朝鮮}} reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and {{ill|Japanese imperialism|ja|日本帝国主義|lt=Japanese imperialism|lt_ja=日本帝国主義者}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
[[ko:낙랑 고분]] {{ill|Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster|ja|楽浪古墳群}} '''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡}}, a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島}} by the {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢|lt=Han dynasty|lt_ja=漢王朝}}’s {{ill|Emperor Wu of Han|ja|武帝 (漢)|lt=Emperor Wu of Han|lt_ja=武帝}} in {{ill|108 BC|ja|紀元前108th year }}.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|{{ill|Encyclopedia of the World|ja|世界大百科事典}}}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡}} corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper={{ill|Dong-A Ilbo|ja|東亜日報}}|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市}} and the {{ill|Lelang area|ja|楽浪区域}}, particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>
== Overview == The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳}} style around the turn from the {{ill|1st century|ja|1世紀}} to the {{ill|2nd century|ja|2世紀}}. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the {{ill|2nd century|ja|2世紀}}, they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|lt=Lelang|lt_ja=楽浪}} and {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡}}.<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after {{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year }}, when {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} came to dominate {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡}} and {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡}}. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in {{ill|South Hwanghae Province|ja|黄海南道}}’s {{ill|Sinchon County|ja|信川郡}} reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in {{ill|404 AD|ja|404th year }} when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher={{ill|National History Compilation Committee|ja|国史編纂委員会}}|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>
Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Wu of Han|ja|武帝 (漢)|lt=Han's Emperor Wu|lt_ja=漢の武帝}}, four commanderies—{{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|lt=Lelang|lt_ja=楽浪}}, {{ill|Xuantu Commandery|ja|玄菟郡|lt=Xuantu|lt_ja=玄菟}}, {{ill|Lintun Commandery|ja|臨屯郡|lt=Lintun|lt_ja=臨屯}}, and {{ill|Zhenfan Commandery|ja|真番郡|lt=Zhenfan|lt_ja=真番}}—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道}} to the {{ill|Yellow Sea|ja|黄海}} and {{ill|Pyongan Province|ja|平安道}}. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, {{ill|Gongsun Kang|ja|公孫康}} established his power in the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東}}, Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡}}. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)|lt=Wei|lt_ja=魏}}, the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Jin dynasty|ja|晋 (王朝)|lt=Jin|lt_ja=晋}}. Meanwhile, with the rise of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|lt=Goryeo|lt_ja=高麗}} in the north and the growing power of {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from {{ill|China|ja|支那}} were stationed, and many {{ill|Han people|ja|漢民族}} settled there, importing Han {{ill|culture|ja|文化}}; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant}} and {{ill|farmers|ja|農民}} migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join {{ill|Murong Chui|ja|慕容廆}} in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興}} era ({{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year }}). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}, which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of {{ill|King Jangsu|ja|長寿王}} after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章}} by {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|lt=Gaozong of Tang|lt_ja=唐の高宗}} of the {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the {{ill|Andong Protectorate|ja|安東都護府}}, difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東}} shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher={{ill|Toyo Bunko|ja|東洋文庫}}|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島}}, namely in the area of the former {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡}}, as well as in the {{ill|Hwangae Area|ja|黄海南道|lt=Hwangae Area|lt_ja=黄海道地域}} where the later-severed {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡}} was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within {{ill|Sunchon City|ja|順川市}}. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from {{ill|North Korea|ja|朝鮮民主主義人民共和国|lt=North Korea|lt_ja=北朝鮮}} reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and {{ill|Japanese imperialism|ja|日本帝国主義|lt=Japanese imperialism|lt_ja=日本帝国主義者}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{ill|Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster|ja|楽浪古墳群}} '''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡}}, a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島}} by the {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢|lt=Han dynasty|lt_ja=漢王朝}}’s {{ill|Emperor Wu of Han|ja|武帝 (漢)|lt=Emperor Wu of Han|lt_ja=武帝}} in {{ill|108 BC|ja|紀元前108th year }}.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|{{ill|Encyclopedia of the World|ja|世界大百科事典}}}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡}} corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper={{ill|Dong-A Ilbo|ja|東亜日報}}|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市}} and the {{ill|Lelang area|ja|楽浪区域}}, particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>
== Overview == The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳}} style around the turn from the {{ill|1st century|ja|1世紀}} to the {{ill|2nd century|ja|2世紀}}. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the {{ill|2nd century|ja|2世紀}}, they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|lt=Lelang|lt_ja=楽浪}} and {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡}}.<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after {{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year }}, when {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}} came to dominate {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡}} and {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡}}. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in {{ill|South Hwanghae Province|ja|黄海南道}}’s {{ill|Sinchon County|ja|信川郡}} reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in {{ill|404 AD|ja|404th year }} when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher={{ill|National History Compilation Committee|ja|国史編纂委員会}}|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>
Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Wu of Han|ja|武帝 (漢)|lt=Han's Emperor Wu|lt_ja=漢の武帝}}, four commanderies—{{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|lt=Lelang|lt_ja=楽浪}}, {{ill|Xuantu Commandery|ja|玄菟郡|lt=Xuantu|lt_ja=玄菟}}, {{ill|Lintun Commandery|ja|臨屯郡|lt=Lintun|lt_ja=臨屯}}, and {{ill|Zhenfan Commandery|ja|真番郡|lt=Zhenfan|lt_ja=真番}}—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道}} to the {{ill|Yellow Sea|ja|黄海}} and {{ill|Pyongan Province|ja|平安道}}. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, {{ill|Gongsun Kang|ja|公孫康}} established his power in the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東}}, Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡}}. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)|lt=Wei|lt_ja=魏}}, the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Jin dynasty|ja|晋 (王朝)|lt=Jin|lt_ja=晋}}. Meanwhile, with the rise of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|lt=Goryeo|lt_ja=高麗}} in the north and the growing power of {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from {{ill|China|ja|支那}} were stationed, and many {{ill|Han people|ja|漢民族}} settled there, importing Han {{ill|culture|ja|文化}}; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant}} and {{ill|farmers|ja|農民}} migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join {{ill|Murong Chui|ja|慕容廆}} in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興}} era ({{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year }}). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}, which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of {{ill|King Jangsu|ja|長寿王}} after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章}} by {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|lt=Gaozong of Tang|lt_ja=唐の高宗}} of the {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the {{ill|Andong Protectorate|ja|安東都護府}}, difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東}} shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher={{ill|Toyo Bunko|ja|東洋文庫}}|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島}}, namely in the area of the former {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡}}, as well as in the {{ill|Hwangae Area|ja|黄海南道|lt=Hwangae Area|lt_ja=黄海道地域}} where the later-severed {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡}} was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within {{ill|Sunchon City|ja|順川市}}. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from {{ill|North Korea|ja|朝鮮民主主義人民共和国|lt=North Korea|lt_ja=北朝鮮}} reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and {{ill|Japanese imperialism|ja|日本帝国主義|lt=Japanese imperialism|lt_ja=日本帝国主義者}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{ill|Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster|ja|楽浪古墳群|12=draft}} '''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}}, a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢|lt=Han dynasty|lt_ja=漢王朝|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Emperor Wu of Han|ja|武帝 (漢)|lt=Emperor Wu of Han|lt_ja=武帝|12=draft}} in {{ill|108 BC|ja|紀元前108th year|12=draft}}.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|{{ill|Encyclopedia of the World|ja|世界大百科事典|12=draft}}}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}} corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=draft}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper={{ill|Dong-A Ilbo|ja|東亜日報|12=draft}}|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Lelang area|ja|楽浪区域|12=draft}}, particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>
== Overview == The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳|12=draft}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=draft}} style around the turn from the {{ill|1st century|ja|1世紀|12=draft}} to the {{ill|2nd century|ja|2世紀|12=draft}}. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=draft}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the {{ill|2nd century|ja|2世紀|12=draft}}, they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|lt=Lelang|lt_ja=楽浪|12=draft}} and {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}}.<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after {{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year|12=draft}}, when {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|12=draft}} came to dominate {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}} and {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}}. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in {{ill|South Hwanghae Province|ja|黄海南道|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Sinchon County|ja|信川郡|12=draft}} reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in {{ill|404 AD|ja|404th year|12=draft}} when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|12=draft}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|12=draft}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|12=draft}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher={{ill|National History Compilation Committee|ja|国史編纂委員会|12=draft}}|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>
Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=draft}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Wu of Han|ja|武帝 (漢)|lt=Han's Emperor Wu|lt_ja=漢の武帝|12=draft}}, four commanderies—{{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|lt=Lelang|lt_ja=楽浪|12=draft}}, {{ill|Xuantu Commandery|ja|玄菟郡|lt=Xuantu|lt_ja=玄菟|12=draft}}, {{ill|Lintun Commandery|ja|臨屯郡|lt=Lintun|lt_ja=臨屯|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Zhenfan Commandery|ja|真番郡|lt=Zhenfan|lt_ja=真番|12=draft}}—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Yellow Sea|ja|黄海|12=draft}} and {{ill|Pyongan Province|ja|平安道|12=draft}}. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gongsun Kang|ja|公孫康|12=draft}} established his power in the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東|12=draft}}, Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}}. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)|lt=Wei|lt_ja=魏|12=draft}}, the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Jin dynasty|ja|晋 (王朝)|lt=Jin|lt_ja=晋|12=draft}}. Meanwhile, with the rise of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|lt=Goryeo|lt_ja=高麗|12=draft}} in the north and the growing power of {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|12=draft}} in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from {{ill|China|ja|支那|12=draft}} were stationed, and many {{ill|Han people|ja|漢民族|12=draft}} settled there, importing Han {{ill|culture|ja|文化|12=draft}}; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=draft}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|12=draft}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=draft}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|12=draft}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|12=draft}} and {{ill|farmers|ja|農民|12=draft}} migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=draft}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|12=draft}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=draft}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|12=draft}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join {{ill|Murong Chui|ja|慕容廆|12=draft}} in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|12=draft}} era ({{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year|12=draft}}). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|12=draft}}, which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of {{ill|King Jangsu|ja|長寿王|12=draft}} after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|12=draft}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|12=draft}} by {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|lt=Gaozong of Tang|lt_ja=唐の高宗|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐|12=draft}}. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the {{ill|Andong Protectorate|ja|安東都護府|12=draft}}, difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東|12=draft}} shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=draft}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher={{ill|Toyo Bunko|ja|東洋文庫|12=draft}}|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|12=draft}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=draft}}, namely in the area of the former {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}}, as well as in the {{ill|Hwangae Area|ja|黄海南道|lt=Hwangae Area|lt_ja=黄海道地域|12=draft}} where the later-severed {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}} was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within {{ill|Sunchon City|ja|順川市|12=draft}}. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from {{ill|North Korea|ja|朝鮮民主主義人民共和国|lt=North Korea|lt_ja=北朝鮮|12=draft}} reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and {{ill|Japanese imperialism|ja|日本帝国主義|lt=Japanese imperialism|lt_ja=日本帝国主義者|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}

== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|12=draft}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=draft}}, namely in the area of the former {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}}, as well as in the {{ill|Hwangae Area|ja|黄海南道|lt=Hwangae Area|lt_ja=黄海道地域|12=draft}} where the later-severed {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}} was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within {{ill|Sunchon City|ja|順川市|12=draft}}. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from {{ill|North Korea|ja|朝鮮民主主義人民共和国|lt=North Korea|lt_ja=北朝鮮|12=draft}} reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and {{ill|Japanese imperialism|ja|日本帝国主義|lt=Japanese imperialism|lt_ja=日本帝国主義者|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{ill|Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster|ja|楽浪古墳群|12=draft}} '''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}}, a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢|lt=Han dynasty|lt_ja=漢王朝|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Emperor Wu of Han|ja|武帝 (漢)|lt=Emperor Wu of Han|lt_ja=武帝|12=draft}} in {{ill|108 BC|ja|紀元前108th year|12=draft}}.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|{{ill|Encyclopedia of the World|ja|世界大百科事典|12=draft}}}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}} corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=draft}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper={{ill|Dong-A Ilbo|ja|東亜日報|12=draft}}|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Lelang area|ja|楽浪区域|12=draft}}, particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>
== Overview == The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳|12=draft}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=draft}} style around the turn from the {{ill|1st century|ja|1世紀|12=draft}} to the {{ill|2nd century|ja|2世紀|12=draft}}. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=draft}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the {{ill|2nd century|ja|2世紀|12=draft}}, they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|lt=Lelang|lt_ja=楽浪|12=draft}} and {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}}.<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after {{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year|12=draft}}, when {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|12=draft}} came to dominate {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}} and {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}}. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in {{ill|South Hwanghae Province|ja|黄海南道|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Sinchon County|ja|信川郡|12=draft}} reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in {{ill|404 AD|ja|404th year|12=draft}} when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|12=draft}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|12=draft}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|12=draft}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher={{ill|National History Compilation Committee|ja|国史編纂委員会|12=draft}}|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>
Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=draft}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Wu of Han|ja|武帝 (漢)|lt=Han's Emperor Wu|lt_ja=漢の武帝|12=draft}}, four commanderies—{{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|lt=Lelang|lt_ja=楽浪|12=draft}}, {{ill|Xuantu Commandery|ja|玄菟郡|lt=Xuantu|lt_ja=玄菟|12=draft}}, {{ill|Lintun Commandery|ja|臨屯郡|lt=Lintun|lt_ja=臨屯|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Zhenfan Commandery|ja|真番郡|lt=Zhenfan|lt_ja=真番|12=draft}}—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Yellow Sea|ja|黄海|12=draft}} and {{ill|Pyongan Province|ja|平安道|12=draft}}. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gongsun Kang|ja|公孫康|12=draft}} established his power in the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東|12=draft}}, Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}}. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)|lt=Wei|lt_ja=魏|12=draft}}, the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Jin dynasty|ja|晋 (王朝)|lt=Jin|lt_ja=晋|12=draft}}. Meanwhile, with the rise of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|lt=Goryeo|lt_ja=高麗|12=draft}} in the north and the growing power of {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|12=draft}} in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from {{ill|China|ja|支那|12=draft}} were stationed, and many {{ill|Han people|ja|漢民族|12=draft}} settled there, importing Han {{ill|culture|ja|文化|12=draft}}; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=draft}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|12=draft}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=draft}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|12=draft}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|12=draft}} and {{ill|farmers|ja|農民|12=draft}} migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=draft}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|12=draft}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=draft}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|12=draft}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join {{ill|Murong Chui|ja|慕容廆|12=draft}} in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|12=draft}} era ({{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year|12=draft}}). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|12=draft}}, which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of {{ill|King Jangsu|ja|長寿王|12=draft}} after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|12=draft}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|12=draft}} by {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|lt=Gaozong of Tang|lt_ja=唐の高宗|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐|12=draft}}. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the {{ill|Andong Protectorate|ja|安東都護府|12=draft}}, difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東|12=draft}} shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=draft}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher={{ill|Toyo Bunko|ja|東洋文庫|12=draft}}|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|12=draft}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=draft}}, namely in the area of the former {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}}, as well as in the {{ill|Hwangae Area|ja|黄海南道|lt=Hwangae Area|lt_ja=黄海道地域|12=draft}} where the later-severed {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}} was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within {{ill|Sunchon City|ja|順川市|12=draft}}. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from {{ill|North Korea|ja|朝鮮民主主義人民共和国|lt=North Korea|lt_ja=北朝鮮|12=draft}} reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and {{ill|Japanese imperialism|ja|日本帝国主義|lt=Japanese imperialism|lt_ja=日本帝国主義者|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>

Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=draft}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|12=draft}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=draft}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|12=draft}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join {{ill|Murong Chui|ja|慕容廆|12=draft}} in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|12=draft}} era ({{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year|12=draft}}). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|12=draft}}, which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of {{ill|King Jangsu|ja|長寿王|12=draft}} after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|12=draft}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|12=draft}} by {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|lt=Gaozong of Tang|lt_ja=唐の高宗|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐|12=draft}}. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the {{ill|Andong Protectorate|ja|安東都護府|12=draft}}, difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東|12=draft}} shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=draft}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher={{ill|Toyo Bunko|ja|東洋文庫|12=draft}}|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|12=draft}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=draft}}, namely in the area of the former {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}}, as well as in the {{ill|Hwangae Area|ja|黄海南道|lt=Hwangae Area|lt_ja=黄海道地域|12=draft}} where the later-severed {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}} was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within {{ill|Sunchon City|ja|順川市|12=draft}}. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from {{ill|North Korea|ja|朝鮮民主主義人民共和国|lt=North Korea|lt_ja=北朝鮮|12=draft}} reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and {{ill|Japanese imperialism|ja|日本帝国主義|lt=Japanese imperialism|lt_ja=日本帝国主義者|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{ill|Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster|ja|楽浪古墳群|12=draft}} '''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}}, a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Han dynasty|ja|漢|lt=Han dynasty|lt_ja=漢王朝|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Emperor Wu of Han|ja|武帝 (漢)|lt=Emperor Wu of Han|lt_ja=武帝|12=draft}} in {{ill|108 BC|ja|紀元前108th year|12=draft}}.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|{{ill|Encyclopedia of the World|ja|世界大百科事典|12=draft}}}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}} corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=draft}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper={{ill|Dong-A Ilbo|ja|東亜日報|12=draft}}|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Lelang area|ja|楽浪区域|12=draft}}, particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>
== Overview == The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳|12=draft}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=draft}} style around the turn from the {{ill|1st century|ja|1世紀|12=draft}} to the {{ill|2nd century|ja|2世紀|12=draft}}. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=draft}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the {{ill|2nd century|ja|2世紀|12=draft}}, they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|lt=Lelang|lt_ja=楽浪|12=draft}} and {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}}.<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after {{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year|12=draft}}, when {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|12=draft}} came to dominate {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}} and {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}}. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in {{ill|South Hwanghae Province|ja|黄海南道|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Sinchon County|ja|信川郡|12=draft}} reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in {{ill|404 AD|ja|404th year|12=draft}} when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|12=draft}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|12=draft}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|12=draft}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher={{ill|National History Compilation Committee|ja|国史編纂委員会|12=draft}}|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>
Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=draft}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Wu of Han|ja|武帝 (漢)|lt=Han's Emperor Wu|lt_ja=漢の武帝|12=draft}}, four commanderies—{{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|lt=Lelang|lt_ja=楽浪|12=draft}}, {{ill|Xuantu Commandery|ja|玄菟郡|lt=Xuantu|lt_ja=玄菟|12=draft}}, {{ill|Lintun Commandery|ja|臨屯郡|lt=Lintun|lt_ja=臨屯|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Zhenfan Commandery|ja|真番郡|lt=Zhenfan|lt_ja=真番|12=draft}}—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Yellow Sea|ja|黄海|12=draft}} and {{ill|Pyongan Province|ja|平安道|12=draft}}. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gongsun Kang|ja|公孫康|12=draft}} established his power in the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東|12=draft}}, Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}}. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)|lt=Wei|lt_ja=魏|12=draft}}, the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Jin dynasty|ja|晋 (王朝)|lt=Jin|lt_ja=晋|12=draft}}. Meanwhile, with the rise of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|lt=Goryeo|lt_ja=高麗|12=draft}} in the north and the growing power of {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|12=draft}} in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from {{ill|China|ja|支那|12=draft}} were stationed, and many {{ill|Han people|ja|漢民族|12=draft}} settled there, importing Han {{ill|culture|ja|文化|12=draft}}; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=draft}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|12=draft}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=draft}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|12=draft}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|12=draft}} and {{ill|farmers|ja|農民|12=draft}} migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=draft}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|12=draft}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=draft}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|12=draft}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join {{ill|Murong Chui|ja|慕容廆|12=draft}} in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|12=draft}} era ({{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year|12=draft}}). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|12=draft}}, which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of {{ill|King Jangsu|ja|長寿王|12=draft}} after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|12=draft}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|12=draft}} by {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|lt=Gaozong of Tang|lt_ja=唐の高宗|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐|12=draft}}. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the {{ill|Andong Protectorate|ja|安東都護府|12=draft}}, difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東|12=draft}} shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=draft}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher={{ill|Toyo Bunko|ja|東洋文庫|12=draft}}|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|12=draft}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=draft}}, namely in the area of the former {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}}, as well as in the {{ill|Hwangae Area|ja|黄海南道|lt=Hwangae Area|lt_ja=黄海道地域|12=draft}} where the later-severed {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}} was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within {{ill|Sunchon City|ja|順川市|12=draft}}. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from {{ill|North Korea|ja|朝鮮民主主義人民共和国|lt=North Korea|lt_ja=北朝鮮|12=draft}} reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and {{ill|Japanese imperialism|ja|日本帝国主義|lt=Japanese imperialism|lt_ja=日本帝国主義者|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>

In the first year of the Jingchu Era of {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)|lt=Wei|lt_ja=魏|12=draft}}, the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Jin dynasty|ja|晋 (王朝)|lt=Jin|lt_ja=晋|12=draft}}. Meanwhile, with the rise of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|lt=Goryeo|lt_ja=高麗|12=draft}} in the north and the growing power of {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済|12=draft}} in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from {{ill|China|ja|支那|12=draft}} were stationed, and many {{ill|Han people|ja|漢民族|12=draft}} settled there, importing Han {{ill|culture|ja|文化|12=draft}}; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=draft}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|12=draft}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=draft}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|12=draft}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|12=draft}} and {{ill|farmers|ja|農民|12=draft}} migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=draft}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|12=draft}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=draft}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|12=draft}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join {{ill|Murong Chui|ja|慕容廆|12=draft}} in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|12=draft}} era ({{ill|313 AD|ja|313rd year|12=draft}}). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗|12=draft}}, which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of {{ill|King Jangsu|ja|長寿王|12=draft}} after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|12=draft}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|12=draft}} by {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|lt=Gaozong of Tang|lt_ja=唐の高宗|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐|12=draft}}. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the {{ill|Andong Protectorate|ja|安東都護府|12=draft}}, difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the {{ill|Liaodong Peninsula|ja|遼東半島|lt=Liaodong|lt_ja=遼東|12=draft}} shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=draft}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher={{ill|Toyo Bunko|ja|東洋文庫|12=draft}}|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|12=draft}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=draft}}, namely in the area of the former {{ill|Lelang Commandery|ja|楽浪郡|12=draft}}, as well as in the {{ill|Hwangae Area|ja|黄海南道|lt=Hwangae Area|lt_ja=黄海道地域|12=draft}} where the later-severed {{ill|Daebang Commandery|ja|帯方郡|12=draft}} was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within {{ill|Sunchon City|ja|順川市|12=draft}}. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from {{ill|North Korea|ja|朝鮮民主主義人民共和国|lt=North Korea|lt_ja=北朝鮮|12=draft}} reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and {{ill|Japanese imperialism|ja|日本帝国主義|lt=Japanese imperialism|lt_ja=日本帝国主義者|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
