revision.text:
'''楽浪古墳群'''（らくろうこふんぐん）は、[[漢|漢王朝]]の[[武帝 (漢)|武帝]]が[[紀元前108年]]に[[朝鮮半島]]に設置した[[植民地]]である[[楽浪郡]]治址の南方から東方にかけて出土する古墳である<ref name="楽浪古墳群">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|[[世界大百科事典]]}}</ref>。学界の通説によると、楽浪郡の位置は、現在の[[平壌直轄市|平壌市]]とその周辺<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=「史料作成時期の読み違い」 ハンバッ大学教授が「万里の長城は平壌まで」説を否定|publisher=|newspaper=[[東亜日報]]|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref>。[[平壌直轄市|平壌市]][[楽浪区域]]貞栢洞付近一帯に千数百基以上の古墳が群集している<ref name="楽浪古墳群"/>。
== 概要 == 楽浪古墳群は、[[1世紀]]から[[2世紀]]ころを境にして、[[木槨墳]]から[[塼築墳]]へと変化する。楽浪古墳群の塼築墳は、概して2世紀ころからつくられているため、楽浪郡・[[帯方郡]]支配層の墳墓と関係がある<ref name="이성제"/>。なかには、[[高句麗]]が楽浪郡・帯方郡を支配するようになった[[313年]]以後につくられたことを示す紀年銘塼が出土している塼築墳もあり、[[黄海南道]][[信川郡]]で出土した塼築墳の紀年銘塼は「永和八年二月四日韓氏造」とあり、高句麗が「[[永楽 (高句麗)|永楽]]」の年号を使用していた[[404年]]につくられたことを示しているが、[[エピタフ|墓誌]]は[[東晋]]の年号を使用しており、楽浪郡・帯方郡遺民が高句麗ではなく、[[中国]]に帰属意識をもっていることが窺える<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=5호16국·남북조 상쟁기 이주민과 고구려·백제|publisher=[[国史編纂委員会]]|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>。
楽浪古墳群の発掘調査を行った[[関野貞]]は、「御承知の通り、[[武帝 (漢)|漢の武帝]]の時、朝鮮の北半部より遼東にかけて其版図に入り、[[楽浪郡|楽浪]][[玄菟郡|玄菟]][[臨屯郡|臨屯]][[真番郡|真番]]の四郡が置かれましたが、其中の楽浪郡は、唯今の[[京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|京畿道]]の一部から[[黄海]][[平安道]]に相当り、漢の勢力の盛衰に隨て、或は其勢力圏外に出て、或は其圏内に入って居りましたが、[[後漢]]末[[遼東半島|遼東]]に[[公孫康]]が勢力を振ひました時、此楽浪郡も其所轄に帰し、[[建安 (漢)|建安]]年中、公孫康は南の一部を割いて[[帯方郡]]を置きました、[[魏 (三国)|魏]]の[[景初]]元年[[公孫氏 (遼東)|公孫氏]]滅びて、楽浪帯方二郡は魏の版図となり引続き[[晋 (王朝)|晋]]の所属に帰しましたが、其間北には[[高句麗|高麗]]が起り南には[[百済]]が勢力を振ひ、両方より侵迫してまいりました為、両郡の勢力はだんだん衰へ、版図を追々縮少し、終に西暦三百十三年の頃楽浪帯方共に亡びてしまいました、此楽浪帯方には[[支那]]から太守を置き、[[漢民族]]も随分来住して漢の[[文化]]を輸入しました、其遺蹟は近年の調査により、平壌を中心として其附近の地に多少発見されました」と報告している<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=[[関野貞]]|date=1914|title=朝鮮に於ける樂浪帯方時代の遺蹟|series=人類學雜誌 29 (10)|publisher=[[日本人類学会]]|page=379}}</ref>。
伊藤一彦は、「楽浪郡には、[[中国]]から[[官吏]]のみならず、多くの[[商人]]や[[農民]]が[[移住]]し、[[中原]]の文化を朝鮮にもたらした。都である平壌からは多数の遺跡・遺物が出土している」と指摘している<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=伊藤一彦|date=2020-03-20|title=7世紀以前の中国・朝鮮関係史|series=経済志林 87 (3･4)|publisher=[[法政大学経済学部学会]]|page=168}}</ref>。
[[今西龍]]は、「此独立的小君主たりし[[張統 (前燕)|張統]]も[[愍帝 (西晋)|晋愍帝]][[建興 (晋)|建興]]元年四月（[[313年]]）に至り其民千余家を帥ひ去て[[慕容廆]]に帰せしかば此時以後[[大同江]]流域は確実に[[高句麗]]の領有となれり。偖て高句麗は第四世紀の初には大同江流域を確実に領有してより平壌には特に重きを置きしものの如かりしに其後百余年を経て[[長寿王]]十五年には遂に平壌に遷都するに至れり。此時既に優秀なる[[中国文明|支那文化]]を摂取し居りし高句麗は更に一層支那文化を大同江流域に輸入し此地に栄へしこと三百余年[[高宗 (唐)|唐の高宗]]の[[総章]]元年唐に滅されたり。唐は此地に[[安東都護府]]を置さ之を直轄せんとせしも事難かりしかば間なく府を[[遼東半島|遼東]]に移せり」と指摘している<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=[[今西龍]]|date=1912-01|title=大同江南の古墳と楽浪王氏との関係|series=東洋学報 2 (1)|publisher=[[東洋文庫]]|page=99}}</ref>。
== 修正主義 == 楽浪古墳群は、[[漢|漢王朝]]が[[朝鮮半島]]に設置した[[植民地]]である[[楽浪郡]]の中心地であった[[平壌直轄市|平壌地域]]、および後に楽浪郡から分離した[[帯方郡]]が位置していた[[黄海道|黄海道地域]]に多数分布する。すなわち、[[楽浪区域]]楽浪洞、斗団洞助王里、南寺里、貞栢洞、[[順川市]]南玉里などである。この発見によって、楽浪郡が平壌付近を支配しており、[[漢|漢王朝]]が朝鮮の発展に巨大な影響を与えた事が強調される<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|和書|title=Constructing "Korean" Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref>。[[朝鮮民主主義人民共和国|北朝鮮]]の学者は、漢王朝の墓を扱うにあたり、それらを高句麗の墓制として再解釈している<ref name="Pai127"/>。中国漢墓と否定できない類似性を持つ遺物のために、北朝鮮の学者は、墳墓の被葬者は高句麗人であるか、または[[偽造]]だとし、「決して墳墓の朝鮮的特性を否定する根拠として解釈すべきではない」と主張する<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>。北朝鮮の学者によると、墳墓を漢王朝の墓とする見方は、朝鮮の[[事大主義|中国事大主義者]]と[[日本帝国主義|日本帝国主義者]]によって捏造された<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:らくろうこふんくん}} [[Category:朝鮮の文化]] [[Category:漢四郡]] [[Category:中国の文化史]] [[Category:平壌市の歴史]] [[Category:朝鮮民主主義人民共和国の古墳]]

'''楽浪古墳群'''（らくろうこふんぐん）は、[[漢|漢王朝]]の[[武帝 (漢)|武帝]]が[[紀元前108年]]に[[朝鮮半島]]に設置した[[植民地]]である[[楽浪郡]]治址の南方から東方にかけて出土する古墳である<ref name="楽浪古墳群">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|[[世界大百科事典]]}}</ref>。学界の通説によると、楽浪郡の位置は、現在の[[平壌直轄市|平壌市]]とその周辺<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=「史料作成時期の読み違い」 ハンバッ大学教授が「万里の長城は平壌まで」説を否定|publisher=|newspaper=[[東亜日報]]|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref>。[[平壌直轄市|平壌市]][[楽浪区域]]貞栢洞付近一帯に千数百基以上の古墳が群集している<ref name="楽浪古墳群"/>。
== 概要 == 楽浪古墳群は、[[1世紀]]から[[2世紀]]ころを境にして、[[木槨墳]]から[[塼築墳]]へと変化する。楽浪古墳群の塼築墳は、概して2世紀ころからつくられているため、楽浪郡・[[帯方郡]]支配層の墳墓と関係がある<ref name="이성제"/>。なかには、[[高句麗]]が楽浪郡・帯方郡を支配するようになった[[313年]]以後につくられたことを示す紀年銘塼が出土している塼築墳もあり、[[黄海南道]][[信川郡]]で出土した塼築墳の紀年銘塼は「永和八年二月四日韓氏造」とあり、高句麗が「[[永楽 (高句麗)|永楽]]」の年号を使用していた[[404年]]につくられたことを示しているが、[[エピタフ|墓誌]]は[[東晋]]の年号を使用しており、楽浪郡・帯方郡遺民が高句麗ではなく、[[中国]]に帰属意識をもっていることが窺える<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=5호16국·남북조 상쟁기 이주민과 고구려·백제|publisher=[[国史編纂委員会]]|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>。
楽浪古墳群の発掘調査を行った[[関野貞]]は、「御承知の通り、[[武帝 (漢)|漢の武帝]]の時、朝鮮の北半部より遼東にかけて其版図に入り、[[楽浪郡|楽浪]][[玄菟郡|玄菟]][[臨屯郡|臨屯]][[真番郡|真番]]の四郡が置かれましたが、其中の楽浪郡は、唯今の[[京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|京畿道]]の一部から[[黄海]][[平安道]]に相当り、漢の勢力の盛衰に隨て、或は其勢力圏外に出て、或は其圏内に入って居りましたが、[[後漢]]末[[遼東半島|遼東]]に[[公孫康]]が勢力を振ひました時、此楽浪郡も其所轄に帰し、[[建安 (漢)|建安]]年中、公孫康は南の一部を割いて[[帯方郡]]を置きました、[[魏 (三国)|魏]]の[[景初]]元年[[公孫氏 (遼東)|公孫氏]]滅びて、楽浪帯方二郡は魏の版図となり引続き[[晋 (王朝)|晋]]の所属に帰しましたが、其間北には[[高句麗|高麗]]が起り南には[[百済]]が勢力を振ひ、両方より侵迫してまいりました為、両郡の勢力はだんだん衰へ、版図を追々縮少し、終に西暦三百十三年の頃楽浪帯方共に亡びてしまいました、此楽浪帯方には[[支那]]から太守を置き、[[漢民族]]も随分来住して漢の[[文化]]を輸入しました、其遺蹟は近年の調査により、平壌を中心として其附近の地に多少発見されました」と報告している<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=[[関野貞]]|date=1914|title=朝鮮に於ける樂浪帯方時代の遺蹟|series=人類學雜誌 29 (10)|publisher=[[日本人類学会]]|page=379}}</ref>。
伊藤一彦は、「楽浪郡には、[[中国]]から[[官吏]]のみならず、多くの[[商人]]や[[農民]]が[[移住]]し、[[中原]]の文化を朝鮮にもたらした。都である平壌からは多数の遺跡・遺物が出土している」と指摘している<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=伊藤一彦|date=2020-03-20|title=7世紀以前の中国・朝鮮関係史|series=経済志林 87 (3･4)|publisher=[[法政大学経済学部学会]]|page=168}}</ref>。
[[今西龍]]は、「此独立的小君主たりし[[張統 (前燕)|張統]]も[[愍帝 (西晋)|晋愍帝]][[建興 (晋)|建興]]元年四月（[[313年]]）に至り其民千余家を帥ひ去て[[慕容廆]]に帰せしかば此時以後[[大同江]]流域は確実に[[高句麗]]の領有となれり。偖て高句麗は第四世紀の初には大同江流域を確実に領有してより平壌には特に重きを置きしものの如かりしに其後百余年を経て[[長寿王]]十五年には遂に平壌に遷都するに至れり。此時既に優秀なる[[中国文明|支那文化]]を摂取し居りし高句麗は更に一層支那文化を大同江流域に輸入し此地に栄へしこと三百余年[[高宗 (唐)|唐の高宗]]の[[総章]]元年唐に滅されたり。唐は此地に[[安東都護府]]を置さ之を直轄せんとせしも事難かりしかば間なく府を[[遼東半島|遼東]]に移せり」と指摘している<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=[[今西龍]]|date=1912-01|title=大同江南の古墳と楽浪王氏との関係|series=東洋学報 2 (1)|publisher=[[東洋文庫]]|page=99}}</ref>。
== 修正主義 == 楽浪古墳群は、[[漢|漢王朝]]が[[朝鮮半島]]に設置した[[植民地]]である[[楽浪郡]]の中心地であった[[平壌直轄市|平壌地域]]、および後に楽浪郡から分離した[[帯方郡]]が位置していた[[黄海道|黄海道地域]]に多数分布する。すなわち、[[楽浪区域]]楽浪洞、斗団洞助王里、南寺里、貞栢洞、[[順川市]]南玉里などである。この発見によって、楽浪郡が平壌付近を支配しており、[[漢|漢王朝]]が朝鮮の発展に巨大な影響を与えた事が強調される<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|和書|title=Constructing "Korean" Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref>。[[朝鮮民主主義人民共和国|北朝鮮]]の学者は、漢王朝の墓を扱うにあたり、それらを高句麗の墓制として再解釈している<ref name="Pai127"/>。中国漢墓と否定できない類似性を持つ遺物のために、北朝鮮の学者は、墳墓の被葬者は高句麗人であるか、または[[偽造]]だとし、「決して墳墓の朝鮮的特性を否定する根拠として解釈すべきではない」と主張する<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>。北朝鮮の学者によると、墳墓を漢王朝の墓とする見方は、朝鮮の[[事大主義|中国事大主義者]]と[[日本帝国主義|日本帝国主義者]]によって捏造された<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:らくろうこふんくん}} [[Category:朝鮮の文化]] [[Category:漢四郡]] [[Category:中国の文化史]] [[Category:平壌市の歴史]] [[Category:朝鮮民主主義人民共和国の古墳]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:楽浪古墳群]][[ko:낙랑 고분]] {{Infobox ancient site}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q12589694]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}} by the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]]’s [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Emperor Wu of Han]] in [[:en:108 BC|108 BC]].<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|[[:en:Heibonsha World Encyclopedia|Encyclopedia of the World]]}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper=[[:en:The Dong-A Ilbo|Dong-A Ilbo]]|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and the [[:en:Rangrang-guyok|Lelang area]], particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>
== Overview == The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokgak Tomb|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style around the turn from the [[:en:1st century|1st century]] to the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]]. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]], they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]].<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after [[:en:313|313 AD]], when [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] came to dominate [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|South Hwanghae Province]]’s [[:en:Sinchon County|Sinchon County]] reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in [[:en:AD 404|404 AD]] when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yongle (Goguryeo)|qq=|1=Yeongnak|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5370698}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Jin|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Eastern Jin dynasty|WD=Q306928}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher=[[:en:National Institute of Korean History|National History Compilation Committee]]|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>
Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|qq=|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Han's Emperor Wu]], four commanderies—[[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]], [[:en:Xuantu Commandery|Xuantu]], [[:en:Lintun Commandery|Lintun]], and [[:en:Zhenfan Commandery|Zhenfan]]—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyeonggi Province|qq=|1=Gyeonggi (historical)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4692210}} to the [[:en:Yellow Sea|Yellow Sea]] and [[:en:Pyongan Province|Pyongan Province]]. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the [[:en:Han_dynasty#Eastern_Han|Later Han]]{{citation needed|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Han|qq=}}, [[:en:Gongsun Kang|Gongsun Kang]] established his power in the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]], Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of [[:en:Cao Wei|Wei]], the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Jin dynasty (266–420)|Jin]]. Meanwhile, with the rise of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goryeo]] in the north and the growing power of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from [[:en:Shina (word)|China]] were stationed, and many [[:en:Han Chinese|Han people]] settled there, importing Han [[:en:Culture|Bunka]]; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|qq=|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Society of Anthropology|qq=|1=Anthropological Society of Nippon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q67201688}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|qq=|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchants|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Peasant|farmers]] migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|qq=|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosei University Faculty of Economics|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|qq=|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Tong|qq=|1=Zhang Tong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12614755}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join [[:en:Murong Hui|Murong Chui]] in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jianxing|qq=|1=Jianxing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6451892}} era ([[:en:313|313 AD]]). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of [[:en:Jangsu of Goguryeo|King Jangsu]] after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese civilization|qq=|1=Chinese civilization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q25558247}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zongzhang|qq=|1=Zongzhang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5154353}} by [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Gaozong of Tang]] of the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]]. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the [[:en:Protectorate General to Pacify the East|Andong Protectorate]], difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]] shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|qq=|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher=[[:en:Tōyō Bunko|Toyo Bunko]]|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang Area|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, namely in the area of the former [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], as well as in the [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|Hwangae Area]] where the later-severed [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]] was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within [[:en:Sunchon|Sunchon City]]. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from [[:en:North Korea|North Korea]] reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and [[:en:Japanese colonial empire|Japanese imperialism]].<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:楽浪古墳群]][[ko:낙랑 고분]] {{Infobox ancient site}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q12589694]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}} by the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]]’s [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Emperor Wu of Han]] in [[:en:108 BC|108 BC]].<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|[[:en:Heibonsha World Encyclopedia|Encyclopedia of the World]]}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper=[[:en:The Dong-A Ilbo|Dong-A Ilbo]]|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and the [[:en:Rangrang-guyok|Lelang area]], particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>
== Overview == The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokgak Tomb|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style around the turn from the [[:en:1st century|1st century]] to the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]]. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]], they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]].<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after [[:en:313|313 AD]], when [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] came to dominate [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|South Hwanghae Province]]’s [[:en:Sinchon County|Sinchon County]] reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in [[:en:AD 404|404 AD]] when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yongle (Goguryeo)|qq=|1=Yeongnak|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5370698}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Jin|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Eastern Jin dynasty|WD=Q306928}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher=[[:en:National Institute of Korean History|National History Compilation Committee]]|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>
Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|qq=|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Han's Emperor Wu]], four commanderies—[[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]], [[:en:Xuantu Commandery|Xuantu]], [[:en:Lintun Commandery|Lintun]], and [[:en:Zhenfan Commandery|Zhenfan]]—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyeonggi Province|qq=|1=Gyeonggi (historical)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4692210}} to the [[:en:Yellow Sea|Yellow Sea]] and [[:en:Pyongan Province|Pyongan Province]]. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the [[:en:Han_dynasty#Eastern_Han|Later Han]]{{citation needed|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Han|qq=}}, [[:en:Gongsun Kang|Gongsun Kang]] established his power in the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]], Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of [[:en:Cao Wei|Wei]], the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Jin dynasty (266–420)|Jin]]. Meanwhile, with the rise of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goryeo]] in the north and the growing power of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from [[:en:Shina (word)|China]] were stationed, and many [[:en:Han Chinese|Han people]] settled there, importing Han [[:en:Culture|Bunka]]; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|qq=|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Society of Anthropology|qq=|1=Anthropological Society of Nippon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q67201688}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|qq=|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchants|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Peasant|farmers]] migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|qq=|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosei University Faculty of Economics|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|qq=|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Tong|qq=|1=Zhang Tong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12614755}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join [[:en:Murong Hui|Murong Chui]] in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jianxing|qq=|1=Jianxing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6451892}} era ([[:en:313|313 AD]]). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of [[:en:Jangsu of Goguryeo|King Jangsu]] after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese civilization|qq=|1=Chinese civilization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q25558247}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zongzhang|qq=|1=Zongzhang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5154353}} by [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Gaozong of Tang]] of the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]]. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the [[:en:Protectorate General to Pacify the East|Andong Protectorate]], difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]] shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|qq=|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher=[[:en:Tōyō Bunko|Toyo Bunko]]|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang Area|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, namely in the area of the former [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], as well as in the [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|Hwangae Area]] where the later-severed [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]] was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within [[:en:Sunchon|Sunchon City]]. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from [[:en:North Korea|North Korea]] reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and [[:en:Japanese colonial empire|Japanese imperialism]].<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:楽浪古墳群]][[ko:낙랑 고분]] {{Infobox ancient site}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q12589694]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}} by the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]]’s [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Emperor Wu of Han]] in [[:en:108 BC|108 BC]].<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|[[:en:Heibonsha World Encyclopedia|Encyclopedia of the World]]}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper=[[:en:The Dong-A Ilbo|Dong-A Ilbo]]|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and the [[:en:Rangrang-guyok|Lelang area]], particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>
== Overview == The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokgak Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style around the turn from the [[:en:1st century|1st century]] to the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]]. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]], they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]].<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after [[:en:313|313 AD]], when [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] came to dominate [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|South Hwanghae Province]]’s [[:en:Sinchon County|Sinchon County]] reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in [[:en:AD 404|404 AD]] when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yongle (Goguryeo)|1=Yeongnak|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5370698}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Jin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Eastern Jin dynasty|WD=Q306928}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher=[[:en:National Institute of Korean History|National History Compilation Committee]]|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>
Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Han's Emperor Wu]], four commanderies—[[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]], [[:en:Xuantu Commandery|Xuantu]], [[:en:Lintun Commandery|Lintun]], and [[:en:Zhenfan Commandery|Zhenfan]]—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyeonggi Province|1=Gyeonggi (historical)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4692210}} to the [[:en:Yellow Sea|Yellow Sea]] and [[:en:Pyongan Province|Pyongan Province]]. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the [[:en:Han_dynasty#Eastern_Han|Later Han]]{{citation needed|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Han|qq=}}, [[:en:Gongsun Kang|Gongsun Kang]] established his power in the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]], Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of [[:en:Cao Wei|Wei]], the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Jin dynasty (266–420)|Jin]]. Meanwhile, with the rise of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goryeo]] in the north and the growing power of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from [[:en:Shina (word)|China]] were stationed, and many [[:en:Han Chinese|Han people]] settled there, importing Han [[:en:Culture|Bunka]]; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Society of Anthropology|1=Anthropological Society of Nippon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q67201688}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchants|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Peasant|farmers]] migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Tong|1=Zhang Tong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12614755}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join [[:en:Murong Hui|Murong Chui]] in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jianxing|1=Jianxing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6451892}} era ([[:en:313|313 AD]]). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of [[:en:Jangsu of Goguryeo|King Jangsu]] after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese civilization|1=Chinese civilization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q25558247}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zongzhang|1=Zongzhang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5154353}} by [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Gaozong of Tang]] of the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]]. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the [[:en:Protectorate General to Pacify the East|Andong Protectorate]], difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]] shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher=[[:en:Tōyō Bunko|Toyo Bunko]]|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang Area|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, namely in the area of the former [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], as well as in the [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|Hwangae Area]] where the later-severed [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]] was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within [[:en:Sunchon|Sunchon City]]. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from [[:en:North Korea|North Korea]] reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and [[:en:Japanese colonial empire|Japanese imperialism]].<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:楽浪古墳群]][[ko:낙랑 고분]] {{Infobox ancient site}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q12589694]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}} by the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]]’s [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Emperor Wu of Han]] in [[:en:108 BC|108 BC]].<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|[[:en:Heibonsha World Encyclopedia|Encyclopedia of the World]]}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper=[[:en:The Dong-A Ilbo|Dong-A Ilbo]]|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and the [[:en:Rangrang-guyok|Lelang area]], particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>
== Overview == The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokgak Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style around the turn from the [[:en:1st century|1st century]] to the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]]. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]], they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]].<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after [[:en:313|313 AD]], when [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] came to dominate [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|South Hwanghae Province]]’s [[:en:Sinchon County|Sinchon County]] reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in [[:en:AD 404|404 AD]] when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yongle (Goguryeo)|1=Yeongnak|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5370698}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Jin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Eastern Jin dynasty|WD=Q306928}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher=[[:en:National Institute of Korean History|National History Compilation Committee]]|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>
Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Han's Emperor Wu]], four commanderies—[[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]], [[:en:Xuantu Commandery|Xuantu]], [[:en:Lintun Commandery|Lintun]], and [[:en:Zhenfan Commandery|Zhenfan]]—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyeonggi Province|1=Gyeonggi (historical)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4692210}} to the [[:en:Yellow Sea|Yellow Sea]] and [[:en:Pyongan Province|Pyongan Province]]. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the [[:en:Han_dynasty#Eastern_Han|Later Han]]{{citation needed|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Han|qq=}}, [[:en:Gongsun Kang|Gongsun Kang]] established his power in the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]], Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of [[:en:Cao Wei|Wei]], the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Jin dynasty (266–420)|Jin]]. Meanwhile, with the rise of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goryeo]] in the north and the growing power of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from [[:en:Shina (word)|China]] were stationed, and many [[:en:Han Chinese|Han people]] settled there, importing Han [[:en:Culture|Bunka]]; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Society of Anthropology|1=Anthropological Society of Nippon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q67201688}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchants|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Peasant|farmers]] migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Tong|1=Zhang Tong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12614755}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join [[:en:Murong Hui|Murong Chui]] in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jianxing|1=Jianxing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6451892}} era ([[:en:313|313 AD]]). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of [[:en:Jangsu of Goguryeo|King Jangsu]] after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese civilization|1=Chinese civilization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q25558247}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zongzhang|1=Zongzhang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5154353}} by [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Gaozong of Tang]] of the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]]. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the [[:en:Protectorate General to Pacify the East|Andong Protectorate]], difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]] shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher=[[:en:Tōyō Bunko|Toyo Bunko]]|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang Area|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, namely in the area of the former [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], as well as in the [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|Hwangae Area]] where the later-severed [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]] was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within [[:en:Sunchon|Sunchon City]]. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from [[:en:North Korea|North Korea]] reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and [[:en:Japanese colonial empire|Japanese imperialism]].<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>

administrative center of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}} by the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]]’s [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Emperor Wu of Han]] in [[:en:108 BC|108 BC]].<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|[[:en:Heibonsha World Encyclopedia|Encyclopedia of the World]]}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper=[[:en:The Dong-A Ilbo|Dong-A Ilbo]]|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and the [[:en:Rangrang-guyok|Lelang area]], particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>
== Overview == The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokgak Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style around the turn from the [[:en:1st century|1st century]] to the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]]. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]], they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]].<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after [[:en:313|313 AD]], when [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] came to dominate [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|South Hwanghae Province]]’s [[:en:Sinchon County|Sinchon County]] reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in [[:en:AD 404|404 AD]] when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yongle (Goguryeo)|1=Yeongnak|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5370698}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Jin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Eastern Jin dynasty|WD=Q306928}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher=[[:en:National Institute of Korean History|National History Compilation Committee]]|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>
Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Han's Emperor Wu]], four commanderies—[[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]], [[:en:Xuantu Commandery|Xuantu]], [[:en:Lintun Commandery|Lintun]], and [[:en:Zhenfan Commandery|Zhenfan]]—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyeonggi Province|1=Gyeonggi (historical)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4692210}} to the [[:en:Yellow Sea|Yellow Sea]] and [[:en:Pyongan Province|Pyongan Province]]. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the [[:en:Han_dynasty#Eastern_Han|Later Han]]{{citation needed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Han|qq=}}, [[:en:Gongsun Kang|Gongsun Kang]] established his power in the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]], Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of [[:en:Cao Wei|Wei]], the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Jin dynasty (266–420)|Jin]]. Meanwhile, with the rise of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goryeo]] in the north and the growing power of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from [[:en:Shina (word)|China]] were stationed, and many [[:en:Han Chinese|Han people]] settled there, importing Han [[:en:Culture|Bunka]]; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Society of Anthropology|1=Anthropological Society of Nippon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q67201688}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchants|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Peasant|farmers]] migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Tong|1=Zhang Tong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12614755}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join [[:en:Murong Hui|Murong Chui]] in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jianxing|1=Jianxing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6451892}} era ([[:en:313|313 AD]]). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of [[:en:Jangsu of Goguryeo|King Jangsu]] after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese civilization|1=Chinese civilization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q25558247}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zongzhang|1=Zongzhang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5154353}} by [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Gaozong of Tang]] of the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]]. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the [[:en:Protectorate General to Pacify the East|Andong Protectorate]], difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]] shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher=[[:en:Tōyō Bunko|Toyo Bunko]]|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang Area|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, namely in the area of the former [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], as well as in the [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|Hwangae Area]] where the later-severed [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]] was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within [[:en:Sunchon|Sunchon City]]. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from [[:en:North Korea|North Korea]] reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and [[:en:Japanese colonial empire|Japanese imperialism]].<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>
