<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:楽浪古墳群]][[ko:낙랑 고분]]
{{Infobox ancient site}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q12589694]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}} by the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]]’s [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Emperor Wu of Han]] in [[:en:108 BC|108 BC]].<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|[[:en:Heibonsha World Encyclopedia|Encyclopedia of the World]]}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper=[[:en:The Dong-A Ilbo|Dong-A Ilbo]]|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and the [[:en:Rangrang-guyok|Lelang area]], particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>

== Overview ==
The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokgak Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style around the turn from the [[:en:1st century|1st century]] to the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]]. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]], they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]].<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after [[:en:313|313 AD]], when [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] came to dominate [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|South Hwanghae Province]]’s [[:en:Sinchon County|Sinchon County]] reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in [[:en:AD 404|404 AD]] when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yongle (Goguryeo)|1=Yeongnak|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5370698}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Jin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Eastern Jin dynasty|WD=Q306928}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher=[[:en:National Institute of Korean History|National History Compilation Committee]]|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>

Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Han's Emperor Wu]], four commanderies—[[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]], [[:en:Xuantu Commandery|Xuantu]], [[:en:Lintun Commandery|Lintun]], and [[:en:Zhenfan Commandery|Zhenfan]]—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyeonggi Province|1=Gyeonggi (historical)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4692210}} to the [[:en:Yellow Sea|Yellow Sea]] and [[:en:Pyongan Province|Pyongan Province]]. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the [[:en:Han_dynasty#Eastern_Han|Later Han]]{{citation needed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Han|qq=}}, [[:en:Gongsun Kang|Gongsun Kang]] established his power in the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]], Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of [[:en:Cao Wei|Wei]], the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Jin dynasty (266–420)|Jin]]. Meanwhile, with the rise of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goryeo]] in the north and the growing power of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from [[:en:Shina (word)|China]] were stationed, and many [[:en:Han Chinese|Han people]] settled there, importing Han [[:en:Culture|Bunka]]; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Society of Anthropology|1=Anthropological Society of Nippon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q67201688}}|page=379}}</ref>

Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchants|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Peasant|farmers]] migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|page=168}}</ref>

{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Tong|1=Zhang Tong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12614755}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join [[:en:Murong Hui|Murong Chui]] in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jianxing|1=Jianxing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6451892}} era ([[:en:313|313 AD]]). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of [[:en:Jangsu of Goguryeo|King Jangsu]] after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese civilization|1=Chinese civilization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q25558247}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zongzhang|1=Zongzhang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5154353}} by [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Gaozong of Tang]] of the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]]. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the [[:en:Protectorate General to Pacify the East|Andong Protectorate]], difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]] shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher=[[:en:Tōyō Bunko|Toyo Bunko]]|page=99}}</ref>

== Revisionism ==
The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang Area|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, namely in the area of the former [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], as well as in the [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|Hwangae Area]] where the later-severed [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]] was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within [[:en:Sunchon|Sunchon City]]. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from [[:en:North Korea|North Korea]] reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and [[:en:Japanese colonial empire|Japanese imperialism]].<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Matching pagename on wikidata]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:楽浪古墳群]][[ko:낙랑 고분]]
{{Infobox ancient site}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q12589694]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}} by the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]]’s [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Emperor Wu of Han]] in [[:en:108 BC|108 BC]].<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|[[:en:Heibonsha World Encyclopedia|Encyclopedia of the World]]}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper=[[:en:The Dong-A Ilbo|Dong-A Ilbo]]|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and the [[:en:Rangrang-guyok|Lelang area]], particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>

== Overview ==
The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokgak Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style around the turn from the [[:en:1st century|1st century]] to the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]]. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]], they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]].<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after [[:en:313|313 AD]], when [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] came to dominate [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|South Hwanghae Province]]’s [[:en:Sinchon County|Sinchon County]] reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in [[:en:AD 404|404 AD]] when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yongle (Goguryeo)|1=Yeongnak|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5370698}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Jin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Eastern Jin dynasty|WD=Q306928}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher=[[:en:National Institute of Korean History|National History Compilation Committee]]|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>

Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Han's Emperor Wu]], four commanderies—[[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]], [[:en:Xuantu Commandery|Xuantu]], [[:en:Lintun Commandery|Lintun]], and [[:en:Zhenfan Commandery|Zhenfan]]—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyeonggi Province|1=Gyeonggi (historical)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4692210}} to the [[:en:Yellow Sea|Yellow Sea]] and [[:en:Pyongan Province|Pyongan Province]]. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the [[:en:Han_dynasty#Eastern_Han|Later Han]]{{citation needed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Han|qq=}}, [[:en:Gongsun Kang|Gongsun Kang]] established his power in the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]], Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of [[:en:Cao Wei|Wei]], the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Jin dynasty (266–420)|Jin]]. Meanwhile, with the rise of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goryeo]] in the north and the growing power of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from [[:en:Shina (word)|China]] were stationed, and many [[:en:Han Chinese|Han people]] settled there, importing Han [[:en:Culture|Bunka]]; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Society of Anthropology|1=Anthropological Society of Nippon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q67201688}}|page=379}}</ref>

Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchants|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Peasant|farmers]] migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|page=168}}</ref>

{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Tong|1=Zhang Tong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12614755}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join [[:en:Murong Hui|Murong Chui]] in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jianxing|1=Jianxing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6451892}} era ([[:en:313|313 AD]]). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of [[:en:Jangsu of Goguryeo|King Jangsu]] after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese civilization|1=Chinese civilization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q25558247}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zongzhang|1=Zongzhang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5154353}} by [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Gaozong of Tang]] of the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]]. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the [[:en:Protectorate General to Pacify the East|Andong Protectorate]], difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]] shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher=[[:en:Tōyō Bunko|Toyo Bunko]]|page=99}}</ref>

== Revisionism ==
The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang Area|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, namely in the area of the former [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], as well as in the [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|Hwangae Area]] where the later-severed [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]] was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within [[:en:Sunchon|Sunchon City]]. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from [[:en:North Korea|North Korea]] reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and [[:en:Japanese colonial empire|Japanese imperialism]].<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>

{{Infobox ancient site}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q12589694]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}} by the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]]’s [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Emperor Wu of Han]] in [[:en:108 BC|108 BC]].<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|[[:en:Heibonsha World Encyclopedia|Encyclopedia of the World]]}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper=[[:en:The Dong-A Ilbo|Dong-A Ilbo]]|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and the [[:en:Rangrang-guyok|Lelang area]], particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>

== Overview ==
The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokgak Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style around the turn from the [[:en:1st century|1st century]] to the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]]. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]], they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]].<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after [[:en:313|313 AD]], when [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] came to dominate [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|South Hwanghae Province]]’s [[:en:Sinchon County|Sinchon County]] reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in [[:en:AD 404|404 AD]] when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yongle (Goguryeo)|1=Yeongnak|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5370698}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Jin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Eastern Jin dynasty|WD=Q306928}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher=[[:en:National Institute of Korean History|National History Compilation Committee]]|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>

Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Han's Emperor Wu]], four commanderies—[[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]], [[:en:Xuantu Commandery|Xuantu]], [[:en:Lintun Commandery|Lintun]], and [[:en:Zhenfan Commandery|Zhenfan]]—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyeonggi Province|1=Gyeonggi (historical)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4692210}} to the [[:en:Yellow Sea|Yellow Sea]] and [[:en:Pyongan Province|Pyongan Province]]. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the [[:en:Han_dynasty#Eastern_Han|Later Han]]{{citation needed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Han|qq=}}, [[:en:Gongsun Kang|Gongsun Kang]] established his power in the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]], Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of [[:en:Cao Wei|Wei]], the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Jin dynasty (266–420)|Jin]]. Meanwhile, with the rise of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goryeo]] in the north and the growing power of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from [[:en:Shina (word)|China]] were stationed, and many [[:en:Han Chinese|Han people]] settled there, importing Han [[:en:Culture|Bunka]]; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Society of Anthropology|1=Anthropological Society of Nippon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q67201688}}|page=379}}</ref>

Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchants|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Peasant|farmers]] migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|page=168}}</ref>

{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Tong|1=Zhang Tong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12614755}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join [[:en:Murong Hui|Murong Chui]] in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jianxing|1=Jianxing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6451892}} era ([[:en:313|313 AD]]). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of [[:en:Jangsu of Goguryeo|King Jangsu]] after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese civilization|1=Chinese civilization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q25558247}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zongzhang|1=Zongzhang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5154353}} by [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Gaozong of Tang]] of the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]]. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the [[:en:Protectorate General to Pacify the East|Andong Protectorate]], difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]] shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher=[[:en:Tōyō Bunko|Toyo Bunko]]|page=99}}</ref>

== Revisionism ==
The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang Area|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, namely in the area of the former [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], as well as in the [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|Hwangae Area]] where the later-severed [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]] was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within [[:en:Sunchon|Sunchon City]]. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from [[:en:North Korea|North Korea]] reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and [[:en:Japanese colonial empire|Japanese imperialism]].<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:楽浪古墳群]][[ko:낙랑 고분]] {{Infobox ancient site}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q12589694]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}} by the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]]’s [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Emperor Wu of Han]] in [[:en:108 BC|108 BC]].<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|[[:en:Heibonsha World Encyclopedia|Encyclopedia of the World]]}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper=[[:en:The Dong-A Ilbo|Dong-A Ilbo]]|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and the [[:en:Rangrang-guyok|Lelang area]], particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>
== Overview == The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokgak Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style around the turn from the [[:en:1st century|1st century]] to the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]]. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]], they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]].<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after [[:en:313|313 AD]], when [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] came to dominate [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|South Hwanghae Province]]’s [[:en:Sinchon County|Sinchon County]] reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in [[:en:AD 404|404 AD]] when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yongle (Goguryeo)|1=Yeongnak|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5370698}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Jin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Eastern Jin dynasty|WD=Q306928}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher=[[:en:National Institute of Korean History|National History Compilation Committee]]|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>
Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Han's Emperor Wu]], four commanderies—[[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]], [[:en:Xuantu Commandery|Xuantu]], [[:en:Lintun Commandery|Lintun]], and [[:en:Zhenfan Commandery|Zhenfan]]—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyeonggi Province|1=Gyeonggi (historical)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4692210}} to the [[:en:Yellow Sea|Yellow Sea]] and [[:en:Pyongan Province|Pyongan Province]]. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the [[:en:Han_dynasty#Eastern_Han|Later Han]]{{citation needed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Han|qq=}}, [[:en:Gongsun Kang|Gongsun Kang]] established his power in the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]], Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of [[:en:Cao Wei|Wei]], the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Jin dynasty (266–420)|Jin]]. Meanwhile, with the rise of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goryeo]] in the north and the growing power of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from [[:en:Shina (word)|China]] were stationed, and many [[:en:Han Chinese|Han people]] settled there, importing Han [[:en:Culture|Bunka]]; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Society of Anthropology|1=Anthropological Society of Nippon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q67201688}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchants|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Peasant|farmers]] migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Tong|1=Zhang Tong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12614755}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join [[:en:Murong Hui|Murong Chui]] in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jianxing|1=Jianxing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6451892}} era ([[:en:313|313 AD]]). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of [[:en:Jangsu of Goguryeo|King Jangsu]] after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese civilization|1=Chinese civilization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q25558247}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zongzhang|1=Zongzhang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5154353}} by [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Gaozong of Tang]] of the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]]. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the [[:en:Protectorate General to Pacify the East|Andong Protectorate]], difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]] shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher=[[:en:Tōyō Bunko|Toyo Bunko]]|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang Area|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, namely in the area of the former [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], as well as in the [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|Hwangae Area]] where the later-severed [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]] was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within [[:en:Sunchon|Sunchon City]]. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from [[:en:North Korea|North Korea]] reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and [[:en:Japanese colonial empire|Japanese imperialism]].<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>

refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper=[[:en:The Dong-A Ilbo|Dong-A Ilbo]]|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and the [[:en:Rangrang-guyok|Lelang area]], particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>
== Overview == The tomb mounds of the Lelang cluster underwent a transition from the {{ill|Mokgak Tomb|ja|木槨墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokgak Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style to the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style around the turn from the [[:en:1st century|1st century]] to the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]]. Since the {{ill|Yochiku Tomb|ja|塼築墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yochiku Tomb|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} style in the Lelang tomb mounds was generally constructed from around the [[:en:2nd century|2nd century]], they are believed to be associated with the ruling class of both [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]].<ref name="이성제"/> Among these, there are some Yochiku Tombs from which epitaphs bearing chronological inscriptions have been unearthed that indicate construction after [[:en:313|313 AD]], when [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] came to dominate [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] and [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|South Hwanghae Province]]’s [[:en:Sinchon County|Sinchon County]] reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in [[:en:AD 404|404 AD]] when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yongle (Goguryeo)|1=Yeongnak|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5370698}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Jin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Eastern Jin dynasty|WD=Q306928}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher=[[:en:National Institute of Korean History|National History Compilation Committee]]|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>
Archaeologist {{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}, who conducted the excavation of the Lelang tomb mounds, reported: “As is well known, during the reign of the [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Han's Emperor Wu]], four commanderies—[[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang]], [[:en:Xuantu Commandery|Xuantu]], [[:en:Lintun Commandery|Lintun]], and [[:en:Zhenfan Commandery|Zhenfan]]—were established, but among these, Lelang Commandery roughly corresponded to a region ranging from part of today's {{ill|Gyeonggi Province|ja|京畿道 (朝鮮八道)|lt=Gyeonggi|lt_ja=京畿道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyeonggi Province|1=Gyeonggi (historical)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4692210}} to the [[:en:Yellow Sea|Yellow Sea]] and [[:en:Pyongan Province|Pyongan Province]]. Depending on the rise and fall of Han power, its area sometimes fell outside and at other times within its sphere of influence. When, at the end of the [[:en:Han_dynasty#Eastern_Han|Later Han]]{{citation needed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Han|qq=}}, [[:en:Gongsun Kang|Gongsun Kang]] established his power in the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]], Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. In the first year of the Jingchu Era of [[:en:Cao Wei|Wei]], the Gongsun clan of Liaodong was overthrown, and the two commanderies of Lelang and Daebang became part of Wei’s territory, later reverting to the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Jin dynasty (266–420)|Jin]]. Meanwhile, with the rise of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goryeo]] in the north and the growing power of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] in the south, both states began to encroach, gradually diminishing the territories of these commanderies until both had vanished around AD 313. In Lelang and Daebang, officials from [[:en:Shina (word)|China]] were stationed, and many [[:en:Han Chinese|Han people]] settled there, importing Han [[:en:Culture|Bunka]]; recent investigations have uncovered various relics in and around Pyongyang.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Sadayoshi Sekino|ja|関野貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sadayoshi Sekino|1=Tadashi Sekino|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5371556}}|date=1914|title=The Ruins of the Lelang-Daebang Period in Korea|series=Anthropological Magazine 29 (10)|publisher={{ill|Japanese Society of Anthropology|ja|日本人類学会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Society of Anthropology|1=Anthropological Society of Nippon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q67201688}}|page=379}}</ref>
Scholar {{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}} has pointed out that “in Lelang Commandery not only officials from {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} but also many {{ill|merchants|ja|Merchant|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merchants|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and [[:en:Peasant|farmers]] migrated, bringing the culture of the Central Plains to Korea. Numerous ruins and artifacts have been excavated from Pyongyang, the capital.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Kazuhiko Itō|ja|伊藤一彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuhiko Itō|1=Kazuhiko Itō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379805}}|date=2020-03-20|title=History of China–Korea Relations Prior to the 7th Century|series=Economic Studies 87 (3･4)|publisher={{ill|Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja|法政大学経済学部学会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosei University Faculty of Economics|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|page=168}}</ref>
{{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Tong|1=Zhang Tong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12614755}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join [[:en:Murong Hui|Murong Chui]] in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jianxing|1=Jianxing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6451892}} era ([[:en:313|313 AD]]). From that time onward, the Datong River basin was undoubtedly incorporated into the territory of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], which, having placed particular emphasis on Pyongyang during its earlier period, ultimately transferred its capital to Pyongyang in the 15th year of the reign of [[:en:Jangsu of Goguryeo|King Jangsu]] after more than a century. At that time, Goguryeo, already enriched by the excellent {{ill|Chinese civilization|ja|中国文明|lt=Sinic culture|lt_ja=支那文化|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese civilization|1=Chinese civilization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q25558247}}, further imported Chinese culture into the Datong River basin and prospered for over three hundred years until it was finally overthrown in the first year of {{ill|Zongzhang|ja|総章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zongzhang|1=Zongzhang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5154353}} by [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Gaozong of Tang]] of the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]]. Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the [[:en:Protectorate General to Pacify the East|Andong Protectorate]], difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]] shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher=[[:en:Tōyō Bunko|Toyo Bunko]]|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang Area|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, namely in the area of the former [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], as well as in the [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|Hwangae Area]] where the later-severed [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]] was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within [[:en:Sunchon|Sunchon City]]. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from [[:en:North Korea|North Korea]] reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and [[:en:Japanese colonial empire|Japanese imperialism]].<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:楽浪古墳群]][[ko:낙랑 고분]] {{Infobox ancient site}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q12589694]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster''' (楽浪古墳群) is a cluster of ancient tomb mounds excavated from the southern to eastern areas of the site of the administrative center of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], a colony established on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}} by the [[:en:Han dynasty|Han dynasty]]’s [[:en:Emperor Wu of Han|Emperor Wu of Han]] in [[:en:108 BC|108 BC]].<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster">{{Kotobank|楽浪古墳群|[[:en:Heibonsha World Encyclopedia|Encyclopedia of the World]]}}</ref> According to the prevailing academic theory, the location of [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]] corresponds to present-day {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and its surroundings.<ref>{{Cite news|author=|date=2015-12-14|title=“Misinterpretation of the Creation Date of Historical Materials” – A professor at Hanbock University refutes the theory that “the Great Wall of Myriad Li reaches Pyongyang”|publisher=|newspaper=[[:en:The Dong-A Ilbo|Dong-A Ilbo]]|url=https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221027105756/https://www.donga.com/jp/article/all/20151214/430197/1|archivedate=2022-10-27}}</ref> In the area around {{ill|Pyongyang|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang City|lt_ja=平壌市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} and the [[:en:Rangrang-guyok|Lelang area]], particularly near Jeongbaek-dong, over a thousand tomb mounds are found clustered together.<ref name="Lelang Tomb Mound Cluster"/>

For instance, a chronological inscription on a Yochiku Tomb unearthed in [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|South Hwanghae Province]]’s [[:en:Sinchon County|Sinchon County]] reads “Second day, second month, 8th year of Yonghe,” indicating construction in [[:en:AD 404|404 AD]] when Goguryeo was using the era name {{ill|Yongle (Goguryeo)|ja|永楽 (高句麗)|lt=Yongle (Goguryeo)|lt_ja=永楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yongle (Goguryeo)|1=Yeongnak|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5370698}}, yet the epitaph employs the era name of the {{ill|Eastern Jin|ja|東晋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eastern Jin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Eastern Jin dynasty|WD=Q306928}}, suggesting that the inhabitants of Lelang and Daebang Commandery maintained an identity affiliated not with Goguryeo but with {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}}.<ref name="이성제">{{Cite news|author=이성제|date=|title=Immigrants of the period of contention between the Northern and Southern dynasties in the Six States and their relation to Goguryeo and Baekje|publisher=[[:en:National Institute of Korean History|National History Compilation Committee]]|newspaper=|url=http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20221123050319/http://contents.nahf.or.kr/id/NAHF.edeah.d_0002_0010_0040|archivedate=2022-11-23}}</ref>

When, at the end of the [[:en:Han_dynasty#Eastern_Han|Later Han]]{{citation needed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Later Han|qq=}}, [[:en:Gongsun Kang|Gongsun Kang]] established his power in the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]], Lelang Commandery was incorporated into his domain, and during the Jian'an period of Han, Gongsun Kang partitioned a southern portion to establish [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]]. {{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}} has remarked, “This once independent petty ruler, {{ill|Zhang Tong|ja|張統 (前燕)|lt=Zhang Tong|lt_ja=張統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhang Tong|1=Zhang Tong|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12614755}}, eventually led over a thousand households to join [[:en:Murong Hui|Murong Chui]] in the fourth month of the first year of the {{ill|Jianxing|ja|建興 (晋)|lt=Jianxing|lt_ja=建興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jianxing|1=Jianxing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6451892}} era ([[:en:313|313 AD]]). Although the Tang government attempted to directly administer the area by establishing the [[:en:Protectorate General to Pacify the East|Andong Protectorate]], difficulties forced them to relocate the protectorate to the [[:en:Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]] shortly thereafter.”<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author={{ill|Ryū Konishi|ja|今西龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryū Konishi|1=Ryū Imanishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11265748}}|date=1912-01|title=The Relationship Between the Tombs South of the Datong River and the Lelang Kings|series=Journal of Oriental Studies 2 (1)|publisher=[[:en:Tōyō Bunko|Toyo Bunko]]|page=99}}</ref>
== Revisionism == The Lelang tomb mounds are widely distributed in the {{ill|Pyongyang Area|ja|平壌直轄市|lt=Pyongyang Area|lt_ja=平壌地域|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pyongyang Area|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平壌市|1=Pyongyang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18808}} – the core area of the colony established by the Han dynasty on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}, namely in the area of the former [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], as well as in the [[:en:South Hwanghae Province|Hwangae Area]] where the later-severed [[:en:Daifang Commandery|Daebang Commandery]] was located. In other words, they are found in locations such as Lelang-dong in the Lelang area, Dotun-dong Sukwang-ri, Namsa-ri, Jeongbaek-dong, and in Namseok-ri within [[:en:Sunchon|Sunchon City]]. This discovery emphasizes that Lelang Commandery dominated the area around Pyongyang and underscores the enormous influence the Han dynasty exerted on the development of Korea.<ref name="Pai127">{{Cite book|language=ja|title=Constructing “Korean” Origins: A Critical Review of Archaeology, Historiography, and Racial Myth in Korean State Formation Theories|first=Hyung Il|last=Pai|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=2000|pages=127-129}}</ref> Scholars from [[:en:North Korea|North Korea]] reinterpret these tombs—originally treated as Han dynasty tombs—as part of the Goguryeo burial system.<ref name="Pai127"/> Due to artifacts bearing undeniable similarities to Chinese Han tombs, North Korean scholars contend that the tomb occupants were either Goguryeo people or even forgeries, and they argue that “the tombs’ distinctly Korean characteristics should never be dismissed as evidence to the contrary.”<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|page=509|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref> According to these scholars, the interpretation of the tombs as Han dynasty tombs was a fabrication concocted by sinocentric ideologues and [[:en:Japanese colonial empire|Japanese imperialism]].<ref>{{Cite journal|language=ja|title=An Outline History of Korean Historiography|first=Yŏng-ho|last=Ch'oe|journal=Korean Studies|volume=4|year=1980|pages=23-25|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|doi=10.1353/ks.1980.0003}}</ref>
